original_abstract: This study examines the critical importance of integrating the Philosophy of Christian Religious Education (CRE) into the curricula of theological colleges. While philosophy is sometimes viewed with skepticism or seen as a source of intellectual confusion within religious circles, this research argues that it is a vital tool for developing wisdom, systematic thinking, and a deeper understanding of human nature. Utilizing a qualitative literature review, the paper explores the synergy between philosophical inquiry and biblical theology, asserting that true wisdom is rooted in the character of Jesus Christ. The findings suggest that learning philosophy empowers theology students to move beyond superficial indoctrination toward a "worldviewish" theology that can engage critically with secular ideologies and contemporary societal challenges. By grounding philosophical concepts like metaphysics and epistemology in the authority of Scripture, theological institutions can better equip students to integrate faith and science, foster moral integrity, and execute more effective ecclesiastical leadership. The study concludes that the study of CRE philosophy is not merely an academic exercise but a foundational necessity for shaping the minds and hearts of future Christian leaders.
humanized_abstract: THE PLACE OF THE PHILOSOPHY OF CHRISTIAN RELIGIOUS EDUCATION (CRE) IN THEORETICAL COLLEGES: A CRITIQUE This paper tries to show that Philosophy of CRE should be treated as a critical subject in the curriculum of theoretical colleges. It believes that philosophers are wiser than the religious adherents. Philosophy is highly valued for bringing clarity, intelligence and knowledge of man in general. This paper based on qualitative literature reviews tries to show that philosophical thinking has relation with the biblical theology. Wisdom of God is seen in the person of Jesus Christ, the Saviour of the world. Hence, the student of theology who learns philosophy will graduate far beyond from what the religious adherents can do in terms of having a “worldviewish” theology, and thereby capable of tackling and confronting all the worldly and secular issues and challenges. Therefore, this study argues that Philosophical issues like metaphysics and issues of knowledge (epistemology) should be grounded and buttressed with scripture, in order to make students of theology to appropriate the study of science to life, cultivate moral rectitude and thus effectively discharge their ecclesiastical duties. The study thus sees the study of philosophy of CRE as very critical and necessary for the shaping the minds of the Christian future clergies.
